sentences.text: Well, when I was about eight years old, I remember an incident.
I was going home from school and there was a group of-- f boys with whom I played in a backyard or out on the street.
One of them was the superintendent's son, for instance.
There were-- there were not Jewish, they were all non-Jewish.
And I come home and they-- they're waylaying me and they're beating me up.
And when I finally came to, I said, what's gotten into you?
He says, well, we just learned that you killed Christ-- that you killed Christ.
And I must say to you, that left a profound impact on me.
I didn't know anything about it.
I went up to my father and I said, what is this business about me killing Christ?
I don't remember having done it.

And so my father explained to me that in so much of Christianity, there was this-- at that time-- teaching which has now been declared to be false by the Second Vatican Council that the Jews were responsible for the death of Jesus.
Second Vatican Council in the great document Nostra aetate in 1965 declared that teaching to be false and forbade for Roman Catholics in the future to-- to teach and believe in that.
But in those days, it was not only taught, it was believed in.

I'm completely convinced that it was this particular story that made the Jews responsible for Jesus' death that is the-- the single most powerful seed out of which all anti-Jewish feeling and later anti-Semitism sprouted.
So I got beat up then.
And there were other incidents in-- in school.
I was the only one that was circumcised, of course.
And when we would take showers after gym, for instance, there were many a time when I was made fun of.
I always was always the smallest in the class, so it was fairly easy to do so, though I learned early to take care of myself.
And there were incidents where I-- I even brought a bicycle chain once with me and-- and beat somebody really quite severely because he had attacked me and hurt me.
So there were incidences is what I'm saying.
And then, of course, you know, in 1933, I-- I was-- how old was I?
I was 17 years old.
And I was in high school, of course.
And it needs to be said that even before that-- for instance, on Sunday outings in the youth group that I belonged to-- we would encounter Hitler Youth and there were always fights.
I mean, really sometimes bad fights.
It was-- but we had to take care of ourselves in a way.
I will, however, also say that I remember one time one of the worst beatings I got was from a Jewish group.
They were in the same uniform as those-- as the Nazi group.
These were revisionist young Jews.
They were called Betar.
They were Jabotinsky's and Begin's boys.
And they beat us up and they did a better job than the Nazis.
That was one of the worst beatings that I got.
Well, because there was ideological differences.
We were a-- a Zionist group, leftist, and they were rightist.
They were semi-fascist.
And they wore the same boots, the same brown pants, the same brown shirts.
The only thing they didn't have was the-- was the swastika.
Otherwise, they were totally indistinguishable from the garb that the Hitler Youth were wearing at the time.
So it-- it was- it was [GERMAN], as the saying goes.
Very well.
I mean, first of all, the radio was one of his main instruments.
We, of course, listened.
And we were a politically very alert household.
Two parents like that who certainly stood in life-- Jewish life and public life deeply.
There were many discussions and so my parents' friends would come.
I will never forget my-- my father's colleagues would sometimes come and discuss with him what the latest news meant.
I remember one incident so clearly.
It was the day that the Nazis went into the Ruhr, the German industrialized, demilitarized zone in the-- in the Rhineland, which had technically been under the supervision of the French.
And I still remember my father's colleagues sitting around table.
I was a young man.
I was about 18, I guess, by that time and, of course, was not expected to participate in the conversation.
I was still a child who was to be seen, not heard.
And one of them saying, this is the end of Germany.
The French will never stand for German troops to move into the Ruhr.
They're going to come in force tomorrow or the day after.
It will be the end of this regime and so on.
So these were the-- the feelings in the early days.
There's no question.
Yes.
There were some Jews who thought that they could live with-- the-- the Nazis.
There was a group called after the founder the Naumann group, which had primarily I think followers in Berlin.
They would even meet and give the Nazi salute, the stiff-armed salute, at their meetings.

And they really thought that if they were much like the Nazis or thought much like the Nazis about many issues that they would thereby gain for themselves protection and acceptance because they kidded themselves because the Nazis were really not interested in ideology or in ideas.
They were interested in the racial, biological component, as they called it.
And so they did escape as little as anybody else.
But I remember since I lived in Munich there was the brown house, as it was called, the headquarters of the Nazi Party.
And Hitler and his inner circle not only lived there, but they were often on the street.
Across the street from us was a house in which a man by the name of Ritter von Epp lived, who later became the-- the governor of Bavaria when Hitler came to power.
And Hitler and Goering and Goebbels in their open Mercedes limousines would drive up and visit there.
And I could lean out of the window and just, you know, look down across the street and see these open big touring cars and this whole gang assembling there.
And then I had a-- a personal experience-- actually, two.

On the day that Hitler had become the chancellor of Germany, which was January 30th 1933, a date I won't ever forget, I was coming-- well, it must have been actually the next day because that was the evening.

I was coming from Hebrew school, which was downtown because there were so few Jews in any of the high schools that we were collected centrally and given instruction by a teacher there while, of course, in Bavaria at the time, the Lutherans and the Catholics got their religious instruction right in the high school.
There were, I think, two hours set aside each week it's-- if I remember right.
So I was coming home, and as I was coming near a rather big plaza near the brown house, there was a huge crowd and they were all screaming and shouting.
So I asked somebody what had happened.
I hadn't heard about it.
They told me that they were celebrating that Hitler had ascended to the chancellorship-- the rule of Germany, in fact.
I, of course, then didn't follow that crowd but took a little circuitous route to get home.
But my father then told me that he always bicycled to-- to his place of-- of employment and he told me that he bicycled through a whole mass of people that were also streaming in that direction.
And well, my father was such a feisty little Jew he-- he was never scared.
He just was never scared.
I suppose after he'd been in the trenches in the first World War for two and a-- for four and a half years, there was nothing that would scare you anymore.
Anyhow.
And the second incident that I remember very vividly was at school.
It must have been in 1933, soon after Hitler came to power, that it was ordered that the Nazi flag be raised in the school.
And we all had to stand in the windows and witness the ceremony, and so we did.
And there were five, six in each window and one of them kind of half audibly said, now they're hanging these dirty rags also in our beautiful school.
Well, even before 1933, but certainly immediately thereafter, those members of the class who were in the Hitler Youth came in uniform to school.
And the second tallest fellow, biggest fellow in the class, was a leader in the Hitler Youth movement and he heard that statement.
He grabbed a hold of my neck, turned me around, and said, I heard what you said.
You have exactly 10 seconds to apologize or else I'll go to the Gestapo, the secret police.
And he pulled out-- he had a little, you know, grandfather watch with an open cover and he propped it open.
And the-- the teacher stood there, the professor stood there.
He was totally powerless, he couldn't intervene.
And when the 10 seconds were over, I said to him, I know who said it, but I won't tell you.
And he closed the-- the watch, put it back in his watch pocket, and then hauled off to floor me.
Well, my father have been very foresighted and had engaged a retired police captain to teach me jujitsu.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
So I was very much prepared for that kind of an attack and the next moment this fellow lay on the floor.
I was in high school for two more years.
I was never touched.
I was the only Jew left.
There was another Jew in my class and he was beaten up so regularly that his parents took him to Switzerland to finish school.
But I was never touched, not-- not once.
In fact, even when they had their-- their dance for graduation in May of-- April, 1935, they invited me to come with a Jewish girl, of course, it's the only one I would date anyhow.
And I decided that I would make an appearance and for about eight, 10 minutes the two of us were there.
We once danced one dance and then we left because it was uncomfortable.
But I wanted to show them that I wasn't scared to come even into this lair of theirs.
No.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They were simply powerless, afraid.
And I don't blame them.
The class of about 30, 32, whatever it was had at least 12, 14 Hitler Youth members there.
And that teacher would have gotten into an awful lot of trouble if any of them would have taken it into their minds to cause it.
And there-- there were many incidents of people who denounced and were then hauled off to jail and beaten up or worse.
There were-- there were murders happening right after the Nazis came to power.
I remember the story that two, three Jewish attorneys who had defended communists, for instance, were hunted down in the streets and-- and lynched right then and there, murdered right then and there.
So we knew there was danger.

But then it was so unprecedented that the only thing I can say looking back is that we always felt that we could live with it, that we would outlive this what shall we call it fever of illness that had seized a part of Germany, as we thought at the time.
And the Nazis, of course, were also very cunning.
They understood this totally and they did it bit by bit.
I mean, it was the salami tactic, slice by slice.
And after you'd gotten used to one, them came the next restriction and then after that, an additional one and this lawsuit and this denial.
And it was done so cunningly, so cleverly-- psychologically, really, you might say masterly-- that we felt, well, first of all, you could try to live with it.
We knew enough Jewish history to know the Jews had lived what we thought were under worse conditions, at least initially.
And secondly, we thought it couldn't last anyhow.
How would the world stand for this kind of a government, for this kind of a movement to come to power?
It's the first time in history that a movement came to power who had as one of its major planks the elimination of Jews, at least from their realm.
Because that was certainly what they decided in the early years of their rule.
I had one personal encounter with-- with a-- with Hitler, if you-- I'd call it that.
My school friend and I decided one time to go towards town and there was a-- a little restaurant that served tea and cookies and the like.
It was only a tea-- tea room.
And he, in particular, liked it, had been there are a number of times and said after school one day, why don't we go there and have a glass-- cup tea, a glass tea with each other?
And I thought it was a wonderful idea.
And so we went down there.
It was maybe a half hour walk from school.

And we were sitting there and I had just begun to drink my tea when the door flew open and six or eight very burly SA men in uniform came in, lined up, looked at the room, nodded, and in walked Hitler was sat down at one of the tables to have a glass of tea.
I have to tell you, I didn't stay.
I told my friend, who was the tallest in the class and-- and the exemplar of Nordic view-- appearance.
I said, Albert, you pay.
I'm leaving.
And I sneaked out along the wall.
I still see myself and against the door and went out.
So that was the one time.
And he had already been chancellor.
I mean, this was after '33 that I was in the same room with him.
Yeah.
No.
No.
Having been a veteran, he had been a sergeant in the army, he was at Verdun, that French fortress, for four and a half years.
Over half a million soldiers died and over a million casualties for a total gain of about 100 yards of-- of territory.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] He looked very Jewish.
By the way, people judged that.
He always called himself, he said, I look like a whole synagogue.
But my father would go into a street car after the Nazis came and would take out the Jewish newspaper and open it up.
I was so scared I would go out into the vestibule, ready to jump if anybody did anything to him.
But I-- I wouldn't even sit next to my father because I was so embarrassed.
But this little Jew, he was smaller than I and I'm not tall, would be that feisty.
But that was my old man.
He just did it that way.

Well, as I might tell you later, I had the opportunity and was finally selected for a scholarship to go to the United States to pursue my rabbinic training in Cincinnati at the Hebrew Union College in 1935.

In 1937, which was the time that my youngest brother was to be bar mitzvah, and fulfilling a promise I had given my parents that I would only be gone two years, I came back to Germany in 1937, in the summer.
And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --at that time, having had the opportunity to live here in the United States for two years, I was absolutely sure that my parents ought to emigrate.
And so I-- I urged my family.
And my father had a very simple solution.
He said, you can take your mother and your two brothers.
I'm staying.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he said, I'll get myself a gun and if they should come for me, I'll shoot, kill a few of them.
And then if they get me, they get me.
And of course, my mother wouldn't hear of it.
She said, your father stays, I'm staying.
And I was a student at the Hebrew Union College.
I had no money whatsoever, no relatives.
I was really totally by myself in that regard.
I couldn't do anything for my two younger siblings.
I mean, I was at that time 21, my younger brother was 16, the youngest was 13.
How could I possibly have taken care of them?
And I myself was on a scholarship, total scholarship for everything, you know.
So the family stayed.
When you returned in 1937 after spending two years in the US, were you aware of how things had progressed in Germany?
Oh, no doubt.
Look, by that time, tens of thousands, maybe even a couple of hundred thousand, of German Jews had left already.
See, one of the advantages, if this is the right word, that German Jews had is that it all started there.
And beginning in 1933, it was made clear that they were not to be part of the German future.
And therefore, out of the 570,000 or so Jews that there were in Germany, eventually I think better than 300,000 were able to escape by way of emigration.
And only a minor fraction of the total German Jewish community was exterminated in the camps later.
It was different, let's say, with the French Jewry or Dutch Jewry or, of course, Eastern European Jewry, where the death rate was close to 100%.

And that was due to the fact that they had time-- this was still before the Second World War, which broke out on September 1st, 1939-- so there were virtually six and a half years before the outbreak of the war which made emigration much more difficult thereafter.
The German Jews had the opportunity, especially if they had friends or had the kind of professions or capacities that other countries were willing to welcome, to leave.
And therefore, to-- to suggest to my parents that they should emigrate was perfectly rational from my point of view.
In fact, it was necessary.
It was just that my father was such a patriot and so stubborn.

The fact that they finally emigrated was due to the fact that my father was taken to Dachau concentration camp in Kristallnacht on November 9th, 1938, and then was told that they would release him only when he could prove by a visa and-- and ticket that he had made final arrangements for emigration from Germany.
Oh, yes.
I was functioning as the hazzan at the- one of my buddies' wedding and I was standing under the chuppah when somebody handed me a cable that my mother had sent saying, father in Dachau.
Rescue him.
Signed, Mother.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] First of all, we had heard that there was such a thing as Dachau because that had been in the news somewhere.
But I don't know that we had heard about Kristallnacht yet because there's an eight-hour difference and timing and so forth.
And then sometimes, the news here was really not particularly keyed to events that pertained primarily to Jews.
I mean, don't let's forget the-- the atmosphere in the United States in the '20s and '30s was not really particularly Philo-Semitic.
On the contrary.
Anti-Jewish feelings were the good tone here.
And there were quotas in universities-- Northwestern University, right here in Evanston-- had a quota of 3%, I believe it was, for the student body.
There were no Jewish professors with tenure in the '20s and early '30s.
The first Jewish professor given tenure in the American university was in the middle '30s.
I think it was Lionel Trilling at Columbia, if I remember right.
So the atmosphere here was not exactly so radically different that if something happened to Jews in Germany, the newspapers would have headlines.
At some time, maybe they did.
I don't really remember that.
It's wonderful that memory can be very selective and the capacity to forget is as precious as the capacity to remember.
So I've forgotten a lot.
What did you do when you heard that your father was in Dachau?
Well, it so happened that the then-president of Hebrew Union College, Professor Julian Morgenstern, was in attendance at the wedding.
And I showed it to him because he was the most exalted official that I knew.
And he called the chairman of the board of the Hebrew Union College, who happened to be a Senator Alfred Cohen.
He was a state senator, but he was also the international president of B'nai B'rith, and as such he had contacts in the State Department.
And he called the State Department, and within a couple of hours I got back the message not to worry.
This was not an action directed against my father, that it was a mass action.
The State Department official to whom Senator Cohen spoke assured him that therefore, everything would be OK, I shouldn't worry.
So I had my mother's cable and I had this official reassurance that everything was going to be all right.
Well, it lasted a few days.
Then, of course, the news was coming through.
My wrote me then a letter, too.
My mother left Munich because she was afraid.
And my school buddy, this Nordic tallest boy in the class-- Albert Klein was his name-- had joined the SS, that's the black shirts, mostly in order to camouflage his family, protect them.

And when he heard that my father had been taken to Dachau, he immediately put on his uniform and took my mother and my two brothers to the train station under SS escort, put them on a train to Stuttgart because my mother had a cousin in Stuttgart who was the consul of Panama and whose house in which the consulate was located, was therefore extraterritorial.
And the idea was that that would be inviolate and the Nazis would not touch at that time, as yet, a foreign what shall we call them, representative.
And so my mother and my two younger brothers spent several months there until my father was released from Dachau.
And then in the meantime, efforts were made by my mother, who had two brothers in Brazil, to get them the necessary visa.
Very little, virtually nothing.
The one thing, however, that he did talk about was that one day they were called to line up and the, the big metal that was the center of Dachau at the time.
And the commandant said, you're going to cut this grass.
And there was a big silence.
And so somebody stepped forward and said, sir, where are the instruments, the scythes?
And the commandant says, you've got teeth, haven't you?
And so these leaders of the German community had to get down on their hands and knees and crop the meadow with their teeth.
That was the only thing my father spoke of, really.
